sentences.text: My grandmother.
And I just run up to her, and I absolutely embraced her, and I stayed with her like-- I don't know how long-- but I-- I really felt the familiar face was marvelous.
You know.
And she-- she said to me, you want to be very quiet here.
Don't make noise, don't do anything because, you know, you're only here for a little while.
And I said yes, I understand.
I didn't know.
I thought I might be staying with her but it didn't look like it.
But she said you've got to be good.
Anyway, I stayed about three weeks with her.

And she was quite horrified at the look of me because, I mean-- but they were an elderly couple that was-- actually the couple that was staying there was my auntie's in-laws, my mother's sister's mother and father who were hiding there.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] mother-- my mother's sister's in-laws, yes, and then my grandmother.
So they were the three in-laws, really, staying there.
They were living in [INAUDIBLE], which I don't know where it is, don't ask me.
It was also-- but I know the-- the town.
Where it is situated exactly, I don't know.
And I have never been back there.
I think it was all in that region somewhere there.
And-- so I stayed there for a little while, and it was very vague, in my mind.
I think I felt-- I relaxed there a little bit, and so I don't think I took too much notice of the surroundings.
You know when you feel that there's somebody taking care of you?
So I didn't sort of-- but those people were very nice.
Do you remember ever asking about your parents?
Yes, I always asked.
Am I going to see my mommy and daddy?
Am I going to see?
And they always said maybe one day, always maybe one day.
And then this Mr.
Hertz ?] came one morning and said, we're going to take you now somewhere and you'll be surprised.
But you see, what happened was nobody ever told you anything.
Because if they ever caught a child, they would torture that child till they would tell something.
And something might be just the thing that they-- the hiding places or some connection or a name or something.
So they never, ever said anything until you got to the place.
Wherever you went it was always like a secret mission, which it had to be.
And so this day, back into my little woolen outfit, I went with him, and I went to a place where-- when the door opened-- I saw my mother.
My mother and my father.
And it was like I had been given a new lease of life.
My mother held me, I think, the whole day.
And my father sat with his arms around the three of us-- I-- for as long as I can remember.
It seemed to go on forever, but we had to go to bed at night.
And there was, in that house-- this was in the [? room ?] [INAUDIBLE] in Haarlem-- there was an-- a husband and wife who owned the place, who had a daughter called Rita.
The daughter Rita was nearly engaged to my Uncle Lou, whom they were hiding, and my grandfather on my father's side, whom they were hiding.
My mother was there and my father was there, and now that meant-- And there were only like three rooms upstairs.
There was a lounge room-- a dining-- lounge-dining all in one and a kitchen-- and a toilet downstairs.

Then upstairs there were three rooms, one for my uncle and my grandfather with a double bed, one for the owners of the house, and one was like a small little bedroom with a dining table in it and six chairs.
And in that room is where we lived.
Now upstairs was an attic, and in that attic was my father and my mother.
And then a little place for me as well to sleep.
So the whole thing was that these people had not wanted to hide a child in the first place.
This was my parent's first hiding place and they'd been there all along.
And so when I arrived there and my father and my mother cuddled me and everything, when that wore off they told me, [?
Corrie, ?] nobody speaks loud here.
Everybody speaks in a whisper.
You must never laugh loud, you must never cry.
You must only say yes and no when you're spoken to, and you must be a very good girl.
Otherwise you can't stay here.
So in order to prove to us that you are going to be a good girl-- and we want to keep you here-- please obey to the rules.
So I said, Mama, I'll do anything.
I will not cry.
I will not do anything.
Not-- no-- nothing, so-- which happened.
We sat in that room, and when you talk about the story of Anne Frank, this is where my story comes in, too.
Because that room was lived in from morning to night.
The only time that was when we had to go to the toilet.
If the coast was clear, downstairs was a toilet.
And only if the old gentleman-- Father Visser, we called him.
She was Mother Visser and he was Father Visser.
If he said it was OK and if the coast was clear.
Because they used to get visitors and things.
Life went on downstairs absolutely, as though nothing was happening in the house upstairs.
So there was all that-- there were now three generations.
There was my grandfather, my uncle who was 10 years younger than my father, then my mother and my father, and then me.
Now when you come into a situation like that in a room where you live in from the morning to the night, you get onto each other's nerves regardless of how good you are.
An old man, he wants to do something, you're sitting with your chair this way, he doesn't want to move.
I want to go there, he doesn't want me to go.
Then if food came up-- we used to eat upstairs in that room-- if food came up, there was arguments about she's got more than he.
He's got more than you.
Why are you eating this?
Why are you giving your child more than us?
You've [? got ?] more than this.
Like-- this went on because there was nothing else to do, only to look at one another and to see that-- and yet, we loved each other and we wanted to make it, but it was like a-- a terrible tension.
My mother taught me how to knit, my mother taught me how to sew.
My mother taught me how to draw-- like drawing.
Like, she'd get some thing-- little painting of some magazine or something that the people had downstairs, and I would copy it and I drew.
And I made myself busy all day long, platting mats.
And-- and then one day my grandfather had naughty things, too, because we were all-- all so hungry.
Although these people were quite good-- my-- my parents were happy to be there-- there was also tensions.
My mother had to do certain homely-- homely duties, my mother had to clean for the home.
And I think they also gave money to them, all monetary things.
So it wasn't all free, but there-- it was kind of them to do it.
But there were also things that were being done on the sidelines.
And then one day, like, there was things like there was a birthday.
It was my father's birthday, and we used to get chocolate hail, chocolate sprinkle.
And this was especially for my father's birthday.
I don't know how this happened.
They managed to get it, and they put it on a small cake they made.
First cake I'd seen since the war had started.
These were the Vissers downstairs--
Yeah, yeah.
--who made the cake.
Yes, they made a cake.
And sometimes also-- because it was for all of us, they got coupons.
So they were able to manage sometimes to make a little bit better dinners.
You know.
I mean, it was one of those things where they tried, they tried very hard.
They were decent people.
And what happened then, this-- this cake came up and everybody was happy.
[INAUDIBLE] my father, and, you iknow, singing softly-- everything softly, happy birthday, happy birthday, you know, like that-- and they started cutting, my mother said, I'll cut.
And so she gave the birthday boy a piece, and then she gave her-- her brother-in-law a piece.
And then she gave me a piece, but on my piece she pushed a bit of extra chocolate sprinkle.
Well, you would have thought murder was going to take place.
How can you do this?
Give your child more than everybody else?
And you know-- see, that's what happens in an atmosphere when you are so cooped up.
You-- you feel like it's impossible.
And then my grandfather used to-- downstairs, they used to have visitors-- and all of a sudden he would like forget where he was and he would say something.
And I remember, I was sitting one day and I was sitting doing my little embroidery or something and he said, hello?
Something, he-- he-- I don't know what happened.
My mother said be quiet, be quiet, there's people downstairs.
And the men downstairs, Father Visser heard it.
And he said to this person that came in, he said, you know, it's a funny thing.
He said, if you didn't think you were hearing noises, you would-- would think you're hearing noises.
We could hear what he was saying.
He said, like, people are going a bit crazy in this war.
All the time you think there's somebody in the houses, don't you?
Like he covered it.
You know, like, he sort of covered it.
He said, isn't it a crazy thing?
He said, you keep hearing things that are not really there.
And then they had a little granddaughter, and the granddaughter used to always want to come upstairs.
Grandpa, can I go to your bedroom?
Can I go here?
Can I go there?
And the grandfather had said, they're keeping dogs up there.
They keep dogs up in the top, they have special dogs there for the-- because they have to keep an eye if anybody strange comes in the house.
So whenever this child used to start wanting to come up, the father-- Father Visser used to say, you know, the dogs are up there, you realize that?
My father, his father, and my uncle used to start barking.
Barking like proper dogs.
And-- and woo!
And, you know, and the child used to run downstairs and really think that there were dogs locked up in that house.
So we lived in constant kind of fear, but then there was one day where there was-- that area was going to be taken for raid.
They decided that street and surrounding streets, Germans were coming in to check the houses.
Anybody hiding anybody, Jews and non-Jews, they wanted to check it.
So my father said, we-- you know, we better do something here.
What is happening?
Where can we hide?
And Father Visser said, I don't know, and they all started putting their head together.
Anyway, they found in the toilet downstairs, there was only a square-- no bigger than where you actually have your toilet-- and a little surrounding with the wash basin.
And they decided that they're going to-- usually under toilets there's sand, sand and where they have pipes running.
So they decided they're going to cut a piece where the toilet is, wood out of the [? failings, ?] and they're going to make a lid, lift it, and then dig, dig, dig, dig, dig.
And then under that house, we will go in.
When there's a raid, we will all go in there.
So they did that, they-- they worked on it, and I remember first my father and my uncle trying it out on their tummy, sliding down.
Because it was low, low-- not enough to stand in but enough to be crouched.
So they decided also to put a chamber pot in there.
And also we had chamber pots upstairs because we couldn't always come down.
So we always had one in the bedroom and one in the attic.
My uncle had one and we had one.
So they said, we'll take the chamber pot down, and we'll also put blankets on the pipes.
Because if you have noise of any kind, even if somebody just does, mm, it echos on those pipes.
They found out it sounded hollow.
So what they decided was to cover all the pipes with blankets, and something for the floor where there was sand.
And that's what happened.
We heard there was a raid coming, and there was tension.
And I remember doing exactly what my parents were doing, so I followed down, and they said, everybody in there now.
And we had like jackets and a thing, because it was quite cold under there.
And there was some kind of food that we got down-- I don't know, but there was parcels they'd made so that we could each day have something to eat because we didn't know how long that would take.
So we all slid under there-- pitch dark, only torch, two torches.
And the Father Visser covered-- covered the top again and there was [? liner, ?] and they put the [? liner ?] on top.
And that was the hiding place.
And we stayed there for five days.
In that five days there was-- I remember this, because it-- it-- if anything I remember, it was quite horrendous.
Every so often if there was a break and they thought they could do it, they'd put some water down or something to drink.
We had like parcels of something to nibble, dry stuff.
Dry-- I remember dry something.
And all we did was under there, was-- my mother, who was having menstruation, she was the only female-- mature female there.
She had to do all her indignities in front of people.
There was nothing she could do about it.
I saw it all.
She had to do whatever.
And everybody went to the toilet on the chamber pot and had to dig it in the sand.
And there was a stench of something unbelievable in there-- under there.
And it was an indignity, I tell you.
It was something, that-- and I was just like-- like a mute.
Because every time we heard the boots walking on top, there was somebody checking.
Somebody had given some kind of an inkling that they were hiding somebody there.
But every third or fourth day, they would come and knock and open-- open the door.
And open-- right through the house, the boots marching through.
And of course, these people said no.
There's nobody here.
Nobody.
And we were under the-- we were really frightened stiff.
Eventually, that was cleared.
Five days later, they left the area.
They had moments like that where they used to indiscriminately keep on, keep on, keep on, till they actually thought maybe they'll find something.
But they didn't and we came out.
But the-- the family Visser said, we can't keep you.
This is too dangerous.
We can't be caught, because one day, they'll walk in and we will be sitting at the little room, and it'll be the end of it all.
So we said-- they said, you have to go.
So my uncle and my grandfather found a hiding place with a lady.
Her name was Antonia.
And she only kept men.
She was hiding men of all denominations, which--
And so, there was my father, my uncle, and my grandfather went there.
And there was no where for us, my mother and I.
So we said-- she said-- they said, you've got to just go.
You've got to go.
So we walked.
She and I walked, and walked, and walked, and walked, and walked.
And in the end, there was a-- a kind of a canal with the big, like, dike area, and we saw trucks with army personnel coming through.
And my mother said, down here!
Down here!
So we flew down there and we lay up against that dike area, must've been quite a long time, crouched up against it, till all that army personnel trucks passed.
And then she said, look, we can't-- we have nowhere to go.
Where will we go?
I'm going back to that Antonia.
My mother had a very strong character, and she was a wonderful woman.
And she said, we are not going to take no for an answer.
She's got to take us until we find somewhere else.
So she went back to that Antonia, and she knocked on the door.
And she opened and she said, I can't take you.
She said, you will have to take me.
You have to take me and my child until the night is over, and we will find something.
And we asked where my father was.
My father, she showed us.
And she said, I have no room to take anybody else.
This is the only place where I can-- My uncle and my grandfather, they had some other place, we-- I never saw it.
But I saw my grand-- my father floating in a tub.
The houses in Haarlem, too, are also on water, like stilts.
And she had, like, water flowing through under their houses.
In Amsterdam, they've got it, too.
And he was in a steel tub, floating.
It was freezing cold.
And he was floating in this steel tub, sitting there, frozen blue.
And I-- we said, Dad, and he said, Well, this is all there is here.
There's nowhere else.
So he was sitting there because there was too much army and stuff going through there, and they were checking.
So we stayed there for a little while, and then this Antonio-- Antonia said that there was another place.
And we went to this place only for a short while.
It was another house where the people were living.
They only had, like, again, like only a short period.
We were like gypsies, really.
We were-- we were just floating all over the place.
There was no home that you could call your own or say, I'll stay here for a while.
We were lucky in a way, because we weren't-- we hadn't gone to concentration camps.
So we were fighting for our lives.
And we stayed in this place with these people, and they also had a shortage of food.
But I-- I used to be starving, and my dad used to sort of do little things and pinch a little here, and pinch a little there.
And there were carrots and things that I used to eat raw, with-- without the skin even peeled, you know?
There was a story even in the other place which I forgot to tell you, where I used to take the carrots out of the rabbit's cage-- because my father used to do it.
Because they used to feed the rabbit for Christmas so that we'd have a nice Christmas dinner.
But-- and so there were carrots going in there.
My father used to steal them out and give them to me so I could eat them in the nighttime, and I have some kind of nourishment before I went to sleep.
And from there, we had to leave, and we were allocated a place in Haarlem, in the [INAUDIBLE].
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was a very narrow street, like a lane, and we were told to go there.
And Mom, and Dad, and myself, we went there.
I don't know how we risked it.
My mother looked very thin, and my father had false papers.
His name was Johannes Peter Bink, and I think my mother must have had some papers as well, but she was his wife, supposedly, so she must've been Bink as well.
And we arrived in this [INAUDIBLE], and we knocked on the door and a lady opened the door.
And if you imagine-- you can see it here, terrace houses in Paddington-- a terrace house, with a long, long corridor, long corridor.
And at the end of the corridor, bright lights, like as though-- now, we've been darkness.
There's been no lights, electric light.
Always everything was blackened and all, you know?
Holland was in darkness.
All of a sudden, you come into, you walk in, and she said, Come in!
Come on, Simon.
And I looked at my father, my mother, and we said, what is this?
Where have we come to?
We thought they were Germans, because how have they got lights?
And we walked into the room that was [? laden ?] food.
She said, Come on, Simon.
You haven't danced for a long time.
You have to dance with me.
The poor man got the shock of his life.
He said-- her name was Anna.
My god, he said, what have I come to here?
And when we looked up, there were lofters, there's rafters in the ceiling, and there were all men's faces, all looking down.
And they were all non-Jewish men that she was hiding, and her husband, and they had a daughter, [?
Rikya. ?] And I said-- my father said, what is this, Anna?
What is this?
And she said, don't you worry about it, Simon.
Don't you worry about it.
You just sit down and eat.
And there was food, and we were eating, and we were having potato, and soup, and we ate everything.
And-- and we-- we couldn't make it out.
And what it was, they were Communist Party.
Now the Communists played a very big part in the war.
They were opposite to the Germans because of Russia and all this.
But the Communists were very much in the underground movement.
And what had happened, they were hiding everybody under the sun, and they had discovered something, the reason they could do it.
They-- had-- they didn't have electric light.
They had battery-lit lights.
They had food.
They had coal.
They had heating.
The house was in a-- lovely, and all these men were sitting there in attic.
And when-- when something happened, they were all disappearing into the rafter, you know?
But anyway, what had happened was, why they had this plentiful situation was that they-- there was a big German warehouse at the corner of the lane.
And they had duplicated keys, and they had a system going where they could get in.
When there was a change of guard, there was a certain amount of time when they could get in.

And they would rearrange-- They had batteries for lighting, There was, as I said, coal, rice, lots of potatoes, anything that-- beans, dried beans, all these kidney beans, which we ate a lot in Holland.
Everything that the army was getting, tins, tins, and tins, they had it there in that warehouse.
So they decided they're going to steal it, but they're doing it systematically.
And systematically, they were doing it in a beautiful way.
We didn't know that.
They never told us that.
But how we knew was because something happened.
They had a brother-in-law that lived further down the street.
And this brother-in-law decided to get greedy.
They distributed all the food evenly to the families that they belonged to.
And this [?
Rikya, ?] the girl that befriended me, she used to say to me, you know, we can go on the road.
You can come with me.
You can come.
And I went with her.
I  went skipping with her.
I did all sorts of things with her.
And I also learned to steal from her, very beautifully.
Because, even though there was plentiful, she said all these people that had carts on the street, they were all collaborators with the Germans.
And she said, Mom said you never have to worry about taking anything from them.
They've got plenty.
So if you see anything and you ask it, if you say to that piece of fruit, these apples-- she told me like this.
This is in Dutch.
She said, if you speak to the apples and you tell them to go away, and they don't go away, then you just take them.
You've ask them.
They don't go away.
So you take them.
So I-- she used to say, And when he looks the other way, you just pinch and put it on this and run, run for your life.
So I became very smart at this.
I used to say, When are we going out again?
Because I thought it was a game.
I was, you know-- by this time, I was about, I think, about seven and a half, eight.
So I started to get very into this, and I quite thought it was good fun, and I was-- for the first time, I was running on the street with her.
But what had happened was, this brother-in-law got greedy, and he decided that he's going to do this himself for his family.
And these people, this Anna and her husband, had it into a very good method.
And they used to re-arrange the bags back into such a position that nobody would even know there was anything missing.
But this man went one night, unbeknown to anybody.
He had the keys also duplicated, like his brother-in-law.
And he went and he got coal, and he didn't realize that there's a minute hole in the coal bag.
Now, when he dragged it at a certain time, whenever they did this, it left a trail from that warehouse, all the way past the house where we were, all the way to his house.

And it so happened that this people, one of the family members, went outside for some reason or other, to their luck, and they opened the door, and they saw this whole trail of coal, leaving from the warehouse, all the way to this fellow's house, which was not far from where they lived.
Just a few doors up.
There was nearly murder.
They were screaming, yelling, the men up in the loft.
I mean, all these men, they were all there.
God knows how many, nine, 10, 12, I don't know how many there were, all laughing, telling joke.
They said, how can you-- what has happened?
They called the brother-in-law, but at the same time, they started sweeping.
The whole family was out sweeping the street.
Of course, we were scared stiff.
We said, we can't-- no way can we stay here, no way.
We're going to get into--
All this time now, we've been so lucky so far.
We're not going to do this.
So my father said there's some name that he knew of, and it was somebody from his mother's side that lived near the Haarlem [INAUDIBLE].
And he said that he would go and see this person, which is, he had these false papers.
He was going.
And my mother said, don't go.
I'm scared, I'm scared.
Anyway, he went and he got to these people's place, and they said no.
You can't stay here because there's a problem here.
They keep on-- you know, this is too dangerous.
The neighbors are telling about everything, and we've got people watching continually.
But we've got somebody else who is my daughter-in-law's someone, son-in-law, and this one is going to have put you up.
And they live in-- not far from us.
And so that is where.
And their name was Mien and Jan Baay.
And that-- we stayed.
Then, we had to get there.
Now, in order to get there, we-- my father came back to tell us that we had somewhere to live.
And it was getting closer to-- this would been, already, 1944 by now.
So we said, you know, we will have to see how we can do it.
And this Anna and the husband, Neusink, set us up with a cart, one of those carts that you wheel, a three-wheeler cart, and she put an old mattress on it.
And so, we looked like evacuees.
And my father put me on top of the mattress, and my mother, and him, and we started walking.
